abstract:
Deep learning-based generative models have recently demonstrated remarkable potential in the time series domain. However, the performance of deep neural networks in this field remains heavily constrained by the size and consistency of available training data—resources that are often scarce, imbalanced, or subject to strict privacy and regulatory requirements in real-world applications. Data augmentation (DA) has emerged as an essential strategy to mitigate these limitations, ranging from basic techniques like noise injection and permutations to the synthesis of entirely new datasets via advanced generative architectures.

This study provides a comprehensive systematic review and a novel taxonomy of the current state of the art in time series data augmentation. We analyze and categorize a wide spectrum of algorithms, including traditional transformations, Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), and Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs). Beyond a simple survey, this work critically evaluates the efficiency of different model variants, discusses the metrics used to assess synthetic data quality, and identifies the core challenges inherent to each approach. By synthesizing the evolution of these techniques and highlighting the methodologies that yield the most robust results, this study serves as a practical guide for researchers and practitioners navigating the complexities of time series augmentation.
